title: Products of Chern Classes of Matroid Tautological Bundles
speaker: Alex Fink
abstract:
In 2008, looking to bound the face vectors of tropical linear spaces, Speyer introduced the g-invariant of a matroid, defined in terms of exterior powers of tautological bundles on Grassmannians. He proved its coefficients nonnegative for matroids representable in characteristic zero and conjectured this in general. In a pair of recent papers, with Kris Shaw and David Speyer we reduce the question to positivity of the top coefficient, and with Andy Berget we then prove the conjecture for this coefficient.
This pair of talks will aim at presenting, with context, the central combinatorial computation of my proof with Berget: a computation by the fan displacement rule of the product of the total Chern classes of two of the tautological quotient bundles of Berget--Eur--Spink--Tseng. Strata in these intersections are governed by the external activity of a pair of matroids. This provides the relationship between the definition of the g-invariant and the external activity complex that we rely on for handling the nonrepresentable case.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant tautological classes for matroids
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes matroid conventions, realizes a representable matroid from a linear subspace and coordinate hyperplanes, constructs the tautological subbundle and quotient bundle over the permutohedral toric variety, and then replaces them for arbitrary matroids by explicit equivariant K-theory classes satisfying the expected exact-sequence relation.

proof_stages.label: Speyer g-invariant as a subdivision-counting covaluation
proof_stages.summary: Speyer's g-polynomial is defined as a signed generating function of Euler characteristics of exterior powers of the tautological K-classes, and its covaluativity plus positivity of component-leading terms explains why nonnegative coefficients would bound face numbers in matroid polytope subdivisions.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to the top coefficient omega
proof_stages.summary: The positivity problem is reduced, using intersection theory on Bergman fans, to the top coefficient omega of minors; for connected representable matroids this coefficient is rewritten as an Euler characteristic of determinant/top exterior powers and dualized through the tautological exact sequence.

proof_stages.label: Two-linear-space generalization and Kempf collapsing
proof_stages.summary: The single-matroid Euler characteristic is generalized to two linear spaces L1 and L2, and Koszul complexes together with Weyman's geometric method for syzygies and Kempf collapsing encode the desired exterior-power Euler characteristics as bigraded Betti numbers of a two-space orbit-closure variety.

proof_stages.label: Squarefree Gröbner degeneration to an activity complex
proof_stages.summary: A term order is chosen so that the initial ideal of the orbit-closure ideal is squarefree, hence a Stanley-Reisner ideal of a simplicial complex, while Cartwright-Sturmfels-type rigidity ensures the degeneration preserves the relevant shape of the minimal free resolution.

proof_stages.label: Hochster-formula positivity via the Schubert special case
proof_stages.summary: Hochster's formula expresses the relevant Tor groups as homology of links in the simplicial complex, and comparison with the one-line case, namely the matroid Schubert variety studied by Ardila and Boocher, identifies a single homological degree so that the Euler-characteristic sign is positive.

proof_stages.label: Extension to nonrepresentable matroids by bivaluativity
proof_stages.summary: The announced combinatorial continuation is to define the activity complex using Dilworth truncation and prove that its K-polynomial or Hilbert function is valuative in each matroid variable, so the representable-case result extends to all matroids because representable matroids span valuative invariants.

examples.name: The octahedron base polytope of U_{2,4}
examples.note: The uniform matroid U_{2,4} is drawn as an octahedron whose subdivision into two square pyramids and a square wall makes the covaluation and the cell-counting meaning of g=2t+t^2 concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws the U_{2,4} octahedron, subdivides it into two square pyramids and a square wall, lists its indicator-vector vertices, and computes/interprets its g-polynomial as 2t+t^2

examples.name: Direct sums of series-parallel matroids
examples.note: In answering why this covaluation is the useful one, the speaker notes that it gives exactly t^k on direct sums of k series-parallel matroids, illustrating the intended normalization for smallest pieces.
examples.engagement_reason: direct sums of k series-parallel matroids are cited as the cases where the invariant gives t^k, without any construction or calculation on them

passes.reason: Direct sums of series-parallel matroids form a systematic low-complexity class attaining the minimal-looking value g=t^k.

examples.name: Binary matroids with no subdivisions
examples.note: The class of binary matroids is mentioned in response to a question as a broad source of matroid polytopes that cannot be subdivided, reinforcing the distinction between the g-bound and actual attainability.
examples.engagement_reason: binary matroids are only cited via the general fact that every binary matroid has no subdivisions

examples.name: Rank equals corank half-point test for omega
examples.note: When n=2r, omega is described as detecting whether (1/2,...,1/2) lies in the relative interior of the matroid polytope, illustrating the constrained behavior of the top coefficient under duality.
examples.engagement_reason: the rank-equals-corank half-point criterion for omega is stated as a known test, with no verification or derivation carried out

passes.reason: In the rank-equals-corank case omega is tested by the central all-1/2 point of the base polytope, a symmetric diagnostic rather than a counterexample.
